title: The Crypt of Encrypted Shadows
subtitle: Ransomware Attack on Core Infrastructure
description: A devastating ransomware attack strikes the heart of your fortress. Encrypted shadows spread across your domain as a dark sorcerer demands tribute in cursed coin. Your realm's most guarded secrets — the records of your most valued patrons — hang in the balance.

roles.name: Incident Commander
roles.title: War Chief
roles.description: Leads the response team, makes containment and escalation decisions

roles.name: IT Operations Lead
roles.title: Arcane Engineer
roles.description: Provides technical context, assesses system impact, proposes containment actions

roles.name: SOC Analyst
roles.title: Shadow Watcher
roles.description: Interprets alerts, provides forensic indicators, monitors detection tooling

roles.name: Compliance / DPO
roles.title: Keeper of the Codex
roles.description: Assesses regulatory notification obligations, advises on breach classification

roles.name: Legal Counsel
roles.title: Loremaster
roles.description: Advises on legal exposure, privilege, law enforcement engagement

roles.name: Customer Relations
roles.title: Herald of the Realm
roles.description: Manages customer communication strategy, assesses reputational impact

roles.name: Senior Management
roles.title: High Council Elder
roles.description: Provides executive-level decision authority, approves external communications

roles.name: Communications Lead
roles.title: Town Crier
roles.description: Drafts holding statements, manages media and social media response

injects.title: Initial Detection — The First Tremor
injects.narrative:
It is 07:42 UTC on a Tuesday morning. The Security Operations Centre (SOC) receives a Priority 1 alert from the Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) platform. Multiple workstations in the Customer Operations department are exhibiting anomalous behaviour: rapid file encryption across mapped network drives, creation of ransom note text files, and outbound connections to a known command-and-control IP address.

Simultaneously, the IT Service Desk receives three calls from senior account managers reporting that they cannot open customer records. One manager reports seeing a pop-up window demanding payment in cryptocurrency.

The EDR dashboard indicates 14 endpoints are affected and the count is rising. The affected file shares include the S:\CustomerData\ and S:\Compliance\ directories.
facilitator_prompts: Who is responsible for declaring this a security incident and at what severity level?
What is the immediate containment strategy? Should affected endpoints be isolated from the network?
What is the impact on customer-facing services? Can account managers continue to service customers?
Has the SOC confirmed whether the EDR agent is blocking the encryption or merely alerting?
At what point do we escalate to the Incident Commander and convene the Crisis Management Team?
Do we have offline backups of the affected file shares? When were they last tested?

dice_events.description: Roll to determine the effectiveness of initial containment

critical_fail.title: Catastrophic Failure
critical_fail.description: The containment attempt accidentally triggers the ransomware's killswitch — but in reverse. The encryption accelerates to backup systems. 8 additional servers are now compromised, including the email gateway.
critical_fail.modifier: Endpoints affected rises from 14 to 47. Email is now offline.

fail.title: Partial Failure
fail.description: Network isolation is incomplete. Three endpoints in another department, previously unaffected, begin showing encryption activity. The threat actor appears to have a secondary C2 channel.
fail.modifier: 3 additional endpoints compromised in another department.

partial.title: Partial Success
partial.description: Most endpoints are successfully isolated, but the file server continues to show encryption activity. It appears the malware has a scheduled task maintaining persistence.
partial.modifier: Workstations contained but file server still active.

success.title: Successful Containment
success.description: The SOC team swiftly isolates all affected endpoints and blocks the C2 channel. Encryption is halted at 14 endpoints. No further spread detected.
success.modifier: Containment successful. Proceed to investigation phase.

critical_success.title: Critical Success — Master Stroke
critical_success.description: Not only is containment successful, but the SOC analyst identifies the exact phishing email and payload hash. They discover the threat actor's staging server is misconfigured, revealing the decryption key in an exposed directory.
critical_success.modifier: Decryption key potentially recovered. Encryption may be reversible without paying ransom.

random_complications: A junior IT technician, panicking, force-restarts three affected servers, potentially destroying volatile forensic evidence.
The EDR vendor's cloud console is experiencing an outage, limiting visibility into the attack.
An account manager has tweeted about the attack from their personal account: 'Something major going on at the organisation today. Systems down everywhere.'
The building's physical access control system, which shares a network segment, begins behaving erratically.

injects.title: Escalation and Scope Expansion — The Shadow Deepens
injects.narrative:
The SOC has completed initial triage. The attack vector has been identified as a phishing email received by a senior account manager at 07:12 UTC. The email contained a macro-enabled Excel attachment purporting to be a customer account rebalancing report. The malware achieved initial execution via a VBA macro and subsequently moved laterally using harvested domain credentials.

Critically, the SOC has identified outbound data transfer to an external IP address. Approximately 2.3 GB of data from the S:\CustomerData\ directory was exfiltrated between 07:20 and 07:41 UTC before the EDR alert triggered. The directory contains customer identification documents, account statements, tax records, and account valuations for approximately 340 major customers.

A ransom note has been found demanding 50 BTC (approximately £2.1 million at current rates), with a 72-hour payment deadline. The note threatens publication of stolen customer data on a dark web leak site if payment is not made.
facilitator_prompts: This is now a confirmed data breach involving personal data of major customers. What are our GDPR Article 33 obligations and what is the notification timeline?
Do we engage the ICO proactively or wait until we have completed our internal assessment?
What is our position on ransom payment? Who has the authority to make this decision?
Should we engage our cyber insurance provider and their nominated incident response panel firm?
How do we preserve forensic evidence whilst also containing the spread?
What are the regulatory notification requirements for a material operational incident?
Do we need to notify payment processors if any payment-related infrastructure is potentially compromised?

dice_events.description: Roll to determine what forensic evidence is recoverable

critical_fail.title: Evidence Destroyed
critical_fail.description: The ransomware includes an anti-forensics module that has wiped Windows Event Logs and overwritten the MFT on affected systems. Forensic recovery will be extremely limited.
critical_fail.modifier: Investigation timeline extended by 72 hours minimum.

fail.title: Limited Evidence
fail.description: Most forensic artifacts on endpoints are encrypted. Network flow data is available but the proxy logs for the exfiltration window have already rotated due to a misconfigured retention policy.
fail.modifier: Cannot confirm exact data exfiltrated. Must assume worst case.

partial.title: Partial Recovery
partial.description: Memory forensics from two endpoints captured before isolation provide the malware's process tree and network indicators. However, the exact scope of exfiltrated files cannot be determined.
partial.modifier: Good IOCs recovered. Exfiltration scope still uncertain.

success.title: Strong Evidence
success.description: Full network capture from the SIEM provides a complete picture of exfiltrated files. The threat actor's infrastructure is identified and reported to the national cybersecurity centre.
success.modifier: Clear understanding of breach scope. National cybersecurity centre engaged.

critical_success.title: Critical Success — Intelligence Goldmine
critical_success.description: The forensics team discovers the threat actor reused infrastructure from a known APT group. The national cybersecurity centre confirms they are already tracking this group and can provide decryption assistance through a partner agency.
critical_success.modifier: Potential for law enforcement-assisted recovery. Decryption support available.

random_complications: The cyber insurance broker cannot be reached — they are themselves dealing with a separate client incident.
The organisation's external legal counsel has a conflict of interest as they also represent the suspected threat actor's previous victims.
A second ransom note is discovered with a different Bitcoin address and different demands, suggesting two separate threat actors may be involved.
The sector regulator calls proactively — they have received intelligence about attacks on organisations and want a status update.

injects.title: Customer and Media Pressure — The Siege Tightens
injects.narrative:
A journalist from a national news outlet has contacted the Communications team, stating they have received a tip-off about a cyber attack at the organisation. They are requesting comment before a 14:00 UTC publication deadline.

Separately, two major customers have contacted their account managers directly, stating they have received emails from an unknown party claiming to hold their financial records and threatening to release them unless the customers themselves pay a personal ransom of £50,000 each.

The Board Chairman has been informed and is requesting an immediate briefing. The organisation's largest customer, representing £800 million in annual revenue, has scheduled an urgent call with the CEO to discuss the security of their data.
facilitator_prompts: What is our media response strategy? Do we issue a holding statement or a full press release?
How do we communicate with affected customers? Who approves the customer notification and what does it contain?
Customers are being directly extorted. Do we advise them to contact law enforcement? Do we coordinate this centrally?
Should we engage the national cybersecurity centre and/or law enforcement?
How do we manage the Board's expectations whilst the investigation is ongoing?
What is the reputational risk assessment? Could we lose the major customer account?

dice_events.description: Roll to determine the media outcome

critical_fail.title: Media Disaster
critical_fail.description: The holding statement is leaked before approval. A disgruntled former employee contacts the journalist with internal details. The story runs with the headline 'Organisation Left Vault Door Open' and includes accurate technical details.
critical_fail.modifier: Full front-page coverage. Customer confidence severely impacted.

fail.title: Negative Coverage
fail.description: The journalist publishes before the organisation can respond. The article is largely speculative but the organisation's 'no comment' is framed negatively. Social media amplification begins.
fail.modifier: Negative press coverage. Reactive communications required.

partial.title: Controlled Narrative
partial.description: The holding statement is issued in time but the journalist has additional sources. The article is balanced but raises questions about the organisation's cyber security posture.
partial.modifier: Mixed coverage. Follow-up press interest likely.

success.title: Positive Narrative Control
success.description: The Communications team issues a well-crafted holding statement emphasising proactive response and customer protection. The journalist's article frames the organisation as a victim responding professionally.
success.modifier: Positive press framing. Customer confidence maintained.

critical_success.title: Critical Success — Public Relations Victory
critical_success.description: The CEO gives an exclusive interview that is widely praised for transparency and leadership. The organisation is held up as an example of excellent incident response. Two prospective customers reach out, impressed by the handling.
critical_success.modifier: Reputational enhancement. New customer interest generated.

random_complications: A board member, not briefed on the communication strategy, gives an unauthorised interview to a business news channel.
The major customer's own CISO requests a full technical briefing and forensic report within 24 hours as a condition of maintaining the relationship.
A hacktivist group claims responsibility for the attack on social media, but their claim is inconsistent with the known threat actor TTPs.
The ICO calls to confirm they have received a complaint from one of the directly extorted customers.

injects.title: Recovery Decisions — The Path to Restoration
injects.narrative:
The containment team has successfully isolated all affected endpoints and halted lateral movement. The threat actor's C2 channel has been blocked at the perimeter firewall and DNS sinkhole.

However, the IT Operations team reports that backup restoration for the encrypted file shares will take approximately 18 hours due to the volume of data and the need to verify backup integrity. During this period, account managers will have no access to customer records.

The CISO has confirmed that the threat actor likely maintained persistent access for up to 11 days before deploying ransomware, based on initial forensic analysis of authentication logs. The full extent of the compromise is not yet known.

The 72-hour ransom deadline is now at T-70 hours.
facilitator_prompts: With 18 hours of downtime, what is our business continuity strategy for customer servicing?
11 days of persistent access suggests a deeper compromise. What additional systems need forensic review?
Do we engage an external digital forensics firm, or rely on internal capability?
What is the decision-making framework for ransom payment, and who chairs that decision?
How do we ensure the threat actor has not left additional backdoors or persistence mechanisms?
What is our timeline for completing the ICO notification given the evolving scope of the breach?

dice_events.description: Roll to determine the recovery outcome

critical_fail.title: Recovery Failure
critical_fail.description: The backup tapes are found to be corrupted — the threat actor compromised the backup system during their 11-day access window. No viable backups exist for the last 14 days. Critical customer data may be permanently lost unless the ransom is paid.
critical_fail.modifier: No viable backups. Ransom payment decision becomes critical.

fail.title: Slow Recovery
fail.description: Backups are viable but the restoration process reveals additional encrypted files in secondary locations. Full recovery will take 36+ hours. Two critical customer transactions cannot be processed.

partial.title: Partial Recovery
partial.description: Primary file shares are restored within 18 hours as estimated. However, some recent documents (last 3 days) are not in the backup and will need to be recreated manually.
partial.modifier: Major systems restored. Some data recreation needed.

success.title: Full Recovery
success.description: Backups are verified clean and restoration completes in 12 hours — faster than estimated. All customer data is recovered. A full sweep confirms no residual persistence mechanisms.
success.modifier: Full recovery achieved ahead of schedule.

critical_success.title: Critical Success — Total Victory
critical_success.description: Recovery is complete within 8 hours. The forensics team identifies and removes all backdoors. Additionally, they discover the threat actor's operational security mistake that reveals the identity of the criminal group, leading to law enforcement action.
critical_success.modifier: Rapid recovery. Threat actor identified. Law enforcement arrests imminent.

random_complications: The threat actor contacts the organisation directly via a secure messaging service, offering a 'discount' if payment is made within 24 hours.
A second wave of phishing emails targeting staff is detected, suggesting the threat actor is attempting to re-establish access.
The external forensics firm estimates costs of £250,000 for a full investigation. The cyber insurance policy has a £100,000 excess.
A regulator from a jurisdiction where affected customers are resident demands a detailed incident report within 48 hours.

debrief.title: Post-Battle Assessment
questions: Was the ransomware detected and contained effectively?
Were regulatory notification obligations understood and met?
Was the ransom payment decision handled appropriately?
Were customer communications timely and effective?
Were backup and recovery procedures adequate?
What improvements should be made to prevent recurrence?
